content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Nasalization}} is defined as: A defining feature where air passes through the nose and mouth simultaneously.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Nasalization' es: A defining feature where air passes through the nose and mouth simultaneously.
content.usage:
Rule: Nasalization
Description: A defining feature where air passes through the nose and mouth simultaneously.

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Rounded Front Vowels}} is defined as: Vowels like /y/ (*tu*) and /ø/ (*deux*) are produced by rounding the lips while the tongue is in the front of the mouth.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Rounded Front Vowels' es: Vowels like /y/ (*tu*) and /ø/ (*deux*) are produced by rounding the lips while the tongue is in the front of the mouth.
content.usage:
Rule: Rounded Front Vowels
Description: Vowels like /y/ (*tu*) and /ø/ (*deux*) are produced by rounding the lips while the tongue is in the front of the mouth.

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Semi-vowels}} is defined as: French uses three distinct glides: /j/ (*fille*), /w/ (*oui*), and the labio-palatal /ɥ/ (*nuit*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Semi-vowels' es: French uses three distinct glides: /j/ (*fille*), /w/ (*oui*), and the labio-palatal /ɥ/ (*nuit*).
content.usage:
Rule: Semi-vowels
Description: French uses three distinct glides: /j/ (*fille*), /w/ (*oui*), and the labio-palatal /ɥ/ (*nuit*).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::The Uvular /ʁ/}} is defined as: The standard French 'r' is a voiced uvular fricative or approximant.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'The Uvular /ʁ/' es: The standard French 'r' is a voiced uvular fricative or approximant.
content.usage:
Rule: The Uvular /ʁ/
Description: The standard French 'r' is a voiced uvular fricative or approximant.

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Palatal Nasal /ɲ/}} is defined as: Found in words like *montagne*.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Palatal Nasal /ɲ/' es: Found in words like *montagne*.
content.usage:
Rule: Palatal Nasal /ɲ/
Description: Found in words like *montagne*.

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Lack of Aspiration}} is defined as: Unlike English, French voiceless stops (/p, t, k/) are never aspirated.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Lack of Aspiration' es: Unlike English, French voiceless stops (/p, t, k/) are never aspirated.
content.usage:
Rule: Lack of Aspiration
Description: Unlike English, French voiceless stops (/p, t, k/) are never aspirated.

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Liaison}} is defined as: Pronouncing a normally silent final consonant before a word starting with a vowel or mute H (e.g., *les amis* -> [le.z‿a.mi], *c'est un* -> [sɛ.t‿œ̃]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Liaison' es: Pronouncing a normally silent final consonant before a word starting with a vowel or mute H (e.g., *les amis* -> [le.z‿a.mi], *c'est un* -> [sɛ.t‿œ̃]).
content.usage:
Rule: Liaison
Description: Pronouncing a normally silent final consonant before a word starting with a vowel or mute H (e.g., *les amis* -> [le.z‿a.mi], *c'est un* -> [sɛ.t‿œ̃]).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Enchaînement}} is defined as: Carrying over a final *pronounced* consonant to the next vowel with no pause (e.g., *il est* -> [i.lɛ], *avec elle* -> [a.vɛ.kɛl]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Enchaînement' es: Carrying over a final *pronounced* consonant to the next vowel with no pause (e.g., *il est* -> [i.lɛ], *avec elle* -> [a.vɛ.kɛl]).
content.usage:
Rule: Enchaînement
Description: Carrying over a final *pronounced* consonant to the next vowel with no pause (e.g., *il est* -> [i.lɛ], *avec elle* -> [a.vɛ.kɛl]).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Silent Final Letters}} is defined as: Most final consonants are silent (e.g., *parler* [paʁle], *chat* [ʃa]), with exceptions often remembered via the word **c-a-r-e-f-u-l** (C, R, F, L are usually pronounced, e.g., *sac*, *mer*, *chef*, *sel*).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Silent Final Letters' es: Most final consonants are silent (e.g., *parler* [paʁle], *chat* [ʃa]), with exceptions often remembered via the word **c-a-r-e-f-u-l** (C, R, F, L are usually pronounced, e.g., *sac*, *mer*, *chef*, *sel*).
content.usage:
Rule: Silent Final Letters
Description: Most final consonants are silent (e.g., *parler* [paʁle], *chat* [ʃa]), with exceptions often remembered via the word **c-a-r-e-f-u-l** (C, R, F, L are usually pronounced, e.g., *sac*, *mer*, *chef*, *sel*).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::"Ne" Omission}} is defined as: In modern colloquial spoken French, the negation particle *ne* is almost always omitted (e.g., *Je ne sais pas* -> "Je sais pas" [ʒə sɛ pa], *Il n'est pas là* -> "Il est pas là" [i lɛ pa la]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de '"Ne" Omission' es: In modern colloquial spoken French, the negation particle *ne* is almost always omitted (e.g., *Je ne sais pas* -> "Je sais pas" [ʒə sɛ pa], *Il n'est pas là* -> "Il est pas là" [i lɛ pa la]).
content.usage:
Rule: "Ne" Omission
Description: In modern colloquial spoken French, the negation particle *ne* is almost always omitted (e.g., *Je ne sais pas* -> "Je sais pas" [ʒə sɛ pa], *Il n'est pas là* -> "Il est pas là" [i lɛ pa la]).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::E-Caduc (Schwa) Deletion}} is defined as: The unstressed /ə/ is dropped in rapid speech whenever it doesn't create a cluster of three consonants (e.g., *un petit peu* -> "un p'tit peu" [œ̃ pti pø], *je te dis* -> "je t'dis" [ʒtdi] -> [ʃtdi] via voicing assimilation).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'E-Caduc (Schwa) Deletion' es: The unstressed /ə/ is dropped in rapid speech whenever it doesn't create a cluster of three consonants (e.g., *un petit peu* -> "un p'tit peu" [œ̃ pti pø], *je te dis* -> "je t'dis" [ʒtdi] -> [ʃtdi] via voicing assimilation).
content.usage:
Rule: E-Caduc (Schwa) Deletion
Description: The unstressed /ə/ is dropped in rapid speech whenever it doesn't create a cluster of three consonants (e.g., *un petit peu* -> "un p'tit peu" [œ̃ pti pø], *je te dis* -> "je t'dis" [ʒtdi] -> [ʃtdi] via voicing assimilation).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pronoun Contractions & Drops}} is defined as: - **Tu-Contraction**: *Tu* + vowel contracts to *t'* (e.g., *Tu es* -> "T'es" [tɛ], *Tu as* -> "T'as" [ta]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Pronoun Contractions & Drops' es: - **Tu-Contraction**: *Tu* + vowel contracts to *t'* (e.g., *Tu es* -> "T'es" [tɛ], *Tu as* -> "T'as" [ta]).
content.usage:
Rule: Pronoun Contractions & Drops
Description: - **Tu-Contraction**: *Tu* + vowel contracts to *t'* (e.g., *Tu es* -> "T'es" [tɛ], *Tu as* -> "T'as" [ta]).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Il/Elle drops}} is defined as: The liquid /l/ in *il* or *ils* is often dropped before consonants (e.g., *il y a* -> "y'a" [ja], *il fait chaud* -> "i' fait chaud" [i fɛ ʃo], *ils veulent* -> "i' veulent" [i vœl]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Il/Elle drops' es: The liquid /l/ in *il* or *ils* is often dropped before consonants (e.g., *il y a* -> "y'a" [ja], *il fait chaud* -> "i' fait chaud" [i fɛ ʃo], *ils veulent* -> "i' veulent" [i vœl]).
content.usage:
Rule: Il/Elle drops
Description: The liquid /l/ in *il* or *ils* is often dropped before consonants (e.g., *il y a* -> "y'a" [ja], *il fait chaud* -> "i' fait chaud" [i fɛ ʃo], *ils veulent* -> "i' veulent" [i vœl]).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Ce-Contraction}} is defined as: *Qu'est-ce qu'il y a* -> "Qu'est-ce qu'y'a" [kɛskja].
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Ce-Contraction' es: *Qu'est-ce qu'il y a* -> "Qu'est-ce qu'y'a" [kɛskja].
content.usage:
Rule: Ce-Contraction
Description: *Qu'est-ce qu'il y a* -> "Qu'est-ce qu'y'a" [kɛskja].

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Final Liquid Dropping}} is defined as: In casual speech, final liquids /l/ and /ʁ/ in words ending in a consonant + liquid are dropped (e.g., *table* -> "tab'" [tab], *mettre* -> "mett'" [mɛt], *possible* -> "possib'" [pɔsib]).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Final Liquid Dropping' es: In casual speech, final liquids /l/ and /ʁ/ in words ending in a consonant + liquid are dropped (e.g., *table* -> "tab'" [tab], *mettre* -> "mett'" [mɛt], *possible* -> "possib'" [pɔsib]).
content.usage:
Rule: Final Liquid Dropping
Description: In casual speech, final liquids /l/ and /ʁ/ in words ending in a consonant + liquid are dropped (e.g., *table* -> "tab'" [tab], *mettre* -> "mett'" [mɛt], *possible* -> "possib'" [pɔsib]).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Fixed Stress}} is defined as: Stress always falls on the last syllable of a rhythmic group (phrase).
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Fixed Stress' es: Stress always falls on the last syllable of a rhythmic group (phrase).
content.usage:
Rule: Fixed Stress
Description: Stress always falls on the last syllable of a rhythmic group (phrase).

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Syllable-timing}} is defined as: Each syllable is given approximately equal duration.
content.explanation: Phonetics and pronunciation guide for French.
content.spanish: En la fonética de French, la regla de 'Syllable-timing' es: Each syllable is given approximately equal duration.
content.usage:
Rule: Syllable-timing
Description: Each syllable is given approximately equal duration.

content.text: What is the French pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Spoken French Shortcut: Drop "Ne"}}? If you say "ne" in everyday conversation in France, you will sound like a textbook. Drop it! Say "Je sais pas" instead of "Je ne sais pas".
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for French.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de French para 'Spoken French Shortcut: Drop "Ne"'? If you say "ne" in everyday conversation in France, you will sound like a textbook. Drop it! Say "Je sais pas" instead of "Je ne sais pas".
content.usage:
Tip: Spoken French Shortcut: Drop "Ne"
If you say "ne" in everyday conversation in France, you will sound like a textbook. Drop it! Say "Je sais pas" instead of "Je ne sais pas".

content.text: What is the French pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::The "T'es" and "Y'a" Contractions}}? Casual French is heavily contracted. Always contract "tu" before vowels ("T'as faim ?" instead of "Tu as faim ?") and use "y'a" instead of "il y a".
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for French.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de French para 'The "T'es" and "Y'a" Contractions'? Casual French is heavily contracted. Always contract "tu" before vowels ("T'as faim ?" instead of "Tu as faim ?") and use "y'a" instead of "il y a".
content.usage:
Tip: The "T'es" and "Y'a" Contractions
Casual French is heavily contracted. Always contract "tu" before vowels ("T'as faim ?" instead of "Tu as faim ?") and use "y'a" instead of "il y a".

content.text: What is the French pronunciation shortcut/tip for {{c1::Tip}}? Master the `ɥ` sound by saying `i` while rounding your lips into a tight `u` shape.
content.explanation: Pronunciation tip/shortcut for French.
content.spanish: ¿Cuál es el atajo/consejo de pronunciación de French para 'consejo'? Master the `ɥ` sound by saying `i` while rounding your lips into a tight `u` shape.
content.usage:
Tip: Tip
Master the `ɥ` sound by saying `i` while rounding your lips into a tight `u` shape.

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::La Liaison}} is defined as: A normally silent final consonant is pronounced and linked to the initial vowel of the following word (e.g., *les enfants* → /le.zɑ̃.fɑ̃/).
content.explanation: Liaison maintains phonetic flow (enchaînement) by avoiding hiatus (the clashing of two vowels). The silent consonant is resurrected to serve as the onset of the next word's syllable.
content.spanish: La Liaison: Pronunciación de una consonante final muda al unirse con una palabra que empieza por vocal. Ejemplo: "les enfants" → "le-zanfan".
content.usage:
Rule: La Liaison
Examples:
  • les enfants → "le-zanfan"
  • vous avez → "vou-zavez"
  • un petit ami → "un peti-tami"

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Elision of 'E' Muet (Schwa Deletion)}} is defined as: Unstressed mute 'e' is dropped in fast speech, merging consonants (e.g., *je ne sais pas* → "j'sais pas" /ʃe.pa/).
content.explanation: Dropping the schwa /ə/ (e caduc) is the most prominent feature of rapid spoken French, reducing the syllable count and leading to consonant voicing assimilation (e.g., /ʒ/ becomes voiceless /ʃ/ before /s/).
content.spanish: Caída de la 'E' muda: Omisión del sonido schwa francés en el habla rápida, provocando asimilación de consonantes contiguas.
content.usage:
Rule: Elision of 'E' Muet
Examples:
  • je ne sais pas → "j'sais pas" (sounds like "she-pa")
  • petit → "p'tit"
  • tout de suite → "tout'suite"

content.text: In French phonetics, the rule of {{c1::Pronoun Compression}} is defined as: Pronoun groups contract by elision of vowels and merging of syllables in rapid speech (e.g., *il y a* → "y'a" /ja/, *tu as* → "t'as").
content.explanation: Subject and object pronouns before verbs are unstressed functional elements and undergo severe contraction in native conversational speed.
content.spanish: Compresión de Pronombres: Contracción y elisión de vocales en grupos de pronombres rápidos. Ejemplo: "il y a" → "y'a".
content.usage:
Rule: Pronoun Compression
Examples:
  • il y a → "y'a"
  • tu as → "t'as"
  • je suis → "chuis" (/ʃɥi/)

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Nous avons des enfants."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Nou-zavon de-zanfan.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: La Liaison
The normally silent final 's' in 'nous' and 'des' is voiced as [z] and links directly to the initial vowels.
content.spanish: Spanish: Tenemos niños.
content.usage: Formal: Nous avons des enfants. → Connected: Nou-zavon de-zanfan.

content.scenario: Pronunciation Drill: Elision of E Muet 🗣️
content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Je ne sais pas quoi faire."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::J'sais pas quoi faire.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Elision of E Muet
The mute 'e' in 'ne' is dropped, creating a consonant cluster /ʒs/ which assimilates to voiceless [ʃs] ('sh-pais').
content.spanish: Spanish: No sé qué hacer.
content.usage: Formal: Je ne sais pas quoi faire. → Connected: J'sais pas quoi faire.

content.text:
Practice saying this phrase fast: "Il y a un problème."
Connected speech pronunciation: {{c1::Y'a un problème.}}
content.explanation:
Applied Rule: Pronoun Compression
The pronoun sequence 'il y a' is compressed to the single syllable 'y'a' (/ja/) in conversational speech.
content.spanish: Spanish: Hay un problema.
content.usage: Formal: Il y a un problème. → Connected: Y'a un problème.
